title: ABAP OOP — Staff+ Design Patterns
description: Designing large-scale OO ABAP systems — not "what is inheritance" but "how to design extensible, testable enterprise OO systems". Abstract vs interface, factory pattern, dependency injection without Spring, observer pattern, casting rules, and ABAP 7.4+ functional programming.

sections.title: Abstract vs Interface Decision Framework

description.value:
The choice between abstract classes and interfaces in ABAP OOP is one of the most misunderstood design decisions. Staff+ engineers apply a rigorous decision framework based on contract semantics, implementation reuse, and versioning strategy.

Interfaces (INTERFACE) define pure contracts — they carry no implementation and no state. In ABAP, interfaces are the only way to achieve multiple inheritance of type (a class can implement multiple interfaces). Use interfaces when: (1) you need to define a capability contract across unrelated classes (e.g., both a CL_HTTP_CLIENT and a CL_RFC_DESTINATION can implement IF_COMMUNICATION), (2) you need to support polymorphic substitution across class hierarchies that don't share a common ancestor, (3) you want to define a stable published API — adding a method to an interface requires updating all implementations (breaking change).

Abstract classes (CLASS ... ABSTRACT) define partial implementations with abstract methods that subclasses must implement. They can carry state (attributes), default implementations, and protected helper methods. Use abstract classes when: (1) multiple related classes share common state and behavior (template method pattern), (2) you need to define common private/protected helper methods, (3) you want to evolve the API over time — adding a concrete method to an abstract class is not a breaking change. Classic use case: CL_EXITHANDLER BADI implementations often use abstract base classes for shared validation logic.

The ABAP-specific complication: ABAP does not support multiple inheritance. You must choose between interface (pure contract, multiple implements allowed) and abstract class (partial implementation, single inheritance only). The ABAP language also lacks default interface methods (Java 8+) — all interface methods must be implemented in every class. This makes interfaces costly to maintain in ABAP. If you have 50 classes implementing IF_DATA_VALIDATOR and you add a new method, all 50 must be updated. With an abstract class, you provide a default implementation and only override where needed.

Staff+ takeaway: FAANG's "Favor composition over inheritance" applies directly. Use interfaces for capability contracts (what something can do) and abstract classes for implementation reuse (how something works). Frame your ABAP OOP experience: "I refactored a monolithic CL_GLOBAL_VALIDATOR into a strategy pattern with IF_VALIDATION_STRATEGY interface and concrete implementations per document type, enabling testability and extensibility without modifying existing code."

sections.codeBlock:
" Abstract vs Interface decision example

INTERFACE if_persistence.
  METHODS save
    IMPORTING iv_data TYPE any
    RAISING   cx_save_error.
  METHODS load
    EXPORTING et_data TYPE ANY TABLE
    RAISING   cx_load_error.
ENDINTERFACE.

" Interface: unrelated classes share contract
CLASS cl_db_persistence DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_persistence.
ENDCLASS.

" Can also be implemented by cl_file_persistence,
" cl_api_persistence, etc. — unrelated hierarchy

" Abstract class: related classes share state + behavior
CLASS cl_validation_base DEFINITION ABSTRACT.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS validate ABSTRACT
      IMPORTING iv_input TYPE any
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_valid) TYPE abap_bool.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
    " Shared state
    DATA mv_rule_table TYPE TABLE OF zvalidation_rule.
    METHODS load_rules.
ENDCLASS.

" Concrete subclass inherits state + must implement validate
CLASS cl_material_validator DEFINITION
  INHERITING FROM cl_validation_base.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS validate REDEFINITION.
ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use an abstract class or an interface for this API?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Do the implementers share common state or behavior?" If yes, abstract class (inheritance + shared code). If they only share a contract (different implementations, no shared code), use interface.

branches.condition: Implementers share state (attributes) and default behavior
branches.path: Abstract class. Use for template method pattern. Protected attributes + abstract methods. Adding a concrete method is non-breaking. Single inheritance only.

branches.condition: Unrelated classes need the same contract but no shared code
branches.path: Interface. Multiple implements allowed. Pure contract — no state, no implementation. Adding a method breaks all implementations. Use for published APIs and BADIs.

branches.condition: Both shared behavior AND multiple-type contract needed
branches.path: Abstract class implementing interface. Best of both: abstract class provides shared state + default methods. Interface provides the polymorphic contract. Common ABAP pattern for BADI base classes.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: In ABAP, interfaces are the only way to achieve multiple-type inheritance. Use interfaces for stable published APIs. Use abstract classes for internal framework code where adding methods shouldn't break consumers.

sections.title: Factory Pattern & Dynamic Runtime Type Creation

description.value:
ABAP provides powerful runtime type creation capabilities through CREATE OBJECT ... TYPE and dynamic type resolution. Staff+ engineers use these to implement the factory pattern at enterprise scale — enabling decoupled, extensible object creation without compile-time dependencies.

Dynamic type creation: CREATE OBJECT lo_instance TYPE (lv_class_name) creates an object of a class whose name is determined at runtime. Combined with RTTI (CL_ABAP_TYPEDESCR), this enables plugin architectures where new implementations can be added without changing the calling code. The class name can come from a customizing table (e.g., ZIMPL_CLASS mapped to document type), a BADI filter, or a configuration file.

Factory method pattern in ABAP: The classic GoF factory method pattern is straightforward in ABAP. A CL_FACTORY class with a static CREATE( iv_type ) method returns an interface-based instance. The factory encapsulates the creation logic — decision rules, parameter validation, error handling. The key benefit: callers depend only on the interface, not on concrete classes. This enables unit testing via dependency injection (see Section 3) and allows new implementations to be added without recompiling existing code.

Abstract factory for related families: For complex scenarios (e.g., creating document processors + validators + formatters for different document types), use an abstract factory. A CL_DOCUMENT_FACTORY returns a factory for a specific document type, which in turn creates the processor, validator, and formatter. This ensures that related objects are compatible (e.g., a purchase order processor is always paired with a purchase order validator).

Staff+ takeaway: Factory pattern in ABAP is essential for open/closed principle — open for extension, closed for modification. When FAANG asks about extensibility patterns, describe ABAP's dynamic type creation as a form of reflective instantiation: "In ABAP, we achieve plugin extensibility using CREATE OBJECT TYPE with class names stored in customizing tables, enabling runtime polymorphism without compile-time coupling — analogous to Java's Class.forName() or Spring's bean factory."

" Factory method with dynamic type creation
INTERFACE if_validator.
  METHODS validate
    IMPORTING iv_input TYPE any
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_success) TYPE abap_bool
    RAISING   cx_validation_error.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_validator_factory DEFINITION CREATE PRIVATE.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS create
      IMPORTING iv_type TYPE zvalidator_type
      RETURNING VALUE(ro_instance) TYPE REF TO if_validator
      RAISING   cx_invalid_validator.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_validator_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create.
    DATA(lv_class_name) = |ZCL_{ iv_type }_VALIDATOR|.
    CREATE OBJECT ro_instance TYPE (lv_class_name).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Usage — no compile-time dependency on concrete class
DATA(lo_validator) = cl_validator_factory=>create( 'MATERIAL' ).
lo_validator->validate( EXPORTING iv_input = ls_data ).

sections.codeBlock:
" Factory method with dynamic type creation
INTERFACE if_validator.
  METHODS validate
    IMPORTING iv_input TYPE any
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_success) TYPE abap_bool
    RAISING   cx_validation_error.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_validator_factory DEFINITION CREATE PRIVATE.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS create
      IMPORTING iv_type          TYPE zvalidator_type
      RETURNING VALUE(ro_instance) TYPE REF TO if_validator
      RAISING   cx_invalid_validator.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_validator_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create.
    DATA(lv_class_name) = |ZCL_{ iv_type }_VALIDATOR|.
    CREATE OBJECT ro_instance TYPE (lv_class_name).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Usage — no compile-time dependency on concrete class
DATA(lo_validator) = cl_validator_factory=>create( 'MATERIAL' ).
lo_validator->validate( EXPORTING iv_input = ls_data ).

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use a factory pattern for creating this object?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the caller know the exact concrete class at compile time?" If yes, direct instantiation (NEW/ CREATE OBJECT) is simpler. If the class depends on runtime configuration, input data, or customizing, use a factory.

branches.condition: Class determined by customizing table or configuration
branches.path: Factory with dynamic type creation. CREATE OBJECT ro_instance TYPE (lv_class_name). Class name from customizing table. No compile-time dependency. Enables plugin architecture.

branches.condition: Object creation requires complex parameter validation or dependency injection
branches.path: Factory method with constructor injection. Factory validates parameters, creates dependencies, and wires them via constructor injection. Caller gets a fully-initialized object.

branches.condition: Simple creation with known concrete class
branches.path: Direct instantiation (NEW cls( )). No factory overhead. ~1 line of code. Prefer this unless you anticipate needing subclass swapping in the future.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Factory pattern enables the Open/Closed Principle — open for extension (new classes via customizing), closed for modification (no changes to calling code). Essential for SaaS extension frameworks.

sections.title: Dependency Injection in ABAP

description.value:
ABAP has no built-in dependency injection framework (no Spring, no Guice). Staff+ engineers build DI manually using constructor injection and factory patterns, enabling testable, loosely-coupled designs even in the ABAP environment.

Why DI matters in ABAP: Traditional ABAP programs have hard-coded dependencies: direct calls to UPDATE statements, BAPI_CREATE function modules, and COMMIT WORK. These are untestable — you cannot unit test a method that commits database changes. By injecting database and communication dependencies, you create seams where tests can insert mock objects. The key ABAP tools for DI are: IF_OSQL_TEST_DOUBLE (database test doubles), CL_OSQL_REPLACE (dynamic SQL replacement), and interface-based constructor injection.

Constructor injection pattern: Pass dependencies as interface references in the constructor, stored as private attributes. The class only depends on interfaces, not concrete implementations. A factory creates the concrete instances and wires them together. In ABAP, this means: (1) define interface for the dependency, (2) implement the interface in a concrete class, (3) the consuming class receives the interface in its constructor, (4) a factory creates and wires everything together. This is the same pattern as constructor injection in Java or C#.

Test doubles with CL_OSQL_REPLACE: ABAP 7.40+ provides CL_OSQL_REPLACE for swapping database tables in Open SQL statements during testing. Combined with IF_OSQL_TEST_DOUBLE, you can replace a production database table with a test-specific internal table. This enables fast, isolated unit tests without database setup/teardown. The test double intercepts Open SQL statements and redirects them to in-memory internal tables.

Staff+ takeaway: Dependency injection in ABAP demonstrates design maturity. When FAANG asks about testability, describe how you structure ABAP code for testability: "I apply constructor injection with interface-based dependencies, enabling unit testing with test doubles via CL_OSQL_REPLACE. This reduced our regression testing cycle from 4 hours (integration tests) to 15 minutes (unit tests). The pattern is identical to Java DI — just without the framework."

dependencies via constructor</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="248">Test setup: CL_OSQL_REPLACE => redirect Open SQL to test data</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="266">Outcome: Fast, isolated unit tests without database connectivity</text></svg>

" Dependency Injection — constructor pattern
INTERFACE if_database_reader.
  METHODS select_materials
    IMPORTING iv_matnr TYPE matnr
    RETURNING VALUE(rt_materials) TYPE zmaterial_tab.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_material_service DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_db_reader TYPE REF TO if_database_reader.
    METHODS get_material_details
      IMPORTING iv_matnr    TYPE matnr
      RETURNING VALUE(rs_material) TYPE zmaterial.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db_reader TYPE REF TO if_database_reader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_material_service IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    mo_db_reader = io_db_reader.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_material_details.
    DATA(lt_materials) = mo_db_reader->select_materials( iv_matnr ).
    " ... business logic
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Factory wiring
DATA(lo_db_reader) = NEW cl_db_reader( ).
DATA(lo_service)   = NEW cl_material_service( lo_db_reader ).

" Test wiring (with test double)
DATA(lo_test_db) = NEW cl_test_db_reader( ).
lo_service = NEW cl_material_service( lo_test_db ).

sections.codeBlock:
" Dependency Injection — constructor injection pattern
INTERFACE if_database_reader.
  METHODS select_materials
    IMPORTING iv_matnr       TYPE matnr
    RETURNING VALUE(rt_materials) TYPE zmaterial_tab.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_material_service DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_db_reader TYPE REF TO if_database_reader.
    METHODS get_material_details
      IMPORTING iv_matnr       TYPE matnr
      RETURNING VALUE(rs_material) TYPE zmaterial.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db_reader TYPE REF TO if_database_reader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_material_service IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    mo_db_reader = io_db_reader.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_material_details.
    DATA(lt_materials) = mo_db_reader->select_materials( iv_matnr ).
    " ... business logic
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Production wiring
DATA(lo_db_reader) = NEW cl_db_reader( ).
DATA(lo_service)   = NEW cl_material_service( lo_db_reader ).

" Test wiring (with test double)
DATA(lo_test_db) = NEW cl_test_db_reader( ).
lo_service = NEW cl_material_service( lo_test_db ).

adversarialResponse.challenge: "ABAP has no Spring or Guice framework. Dependency injection is over-engineered for ABAP. Just call the function module directly."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "ABAP needs a DI framework. SAP should build one." (defensive, no practical solution)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're right that ABAP lacks a built-in DI container, but the principles still apply and there are pragmatic ABAP-native ways to implement DI. The value isn't the framework — it's the testability. When you CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATE' directly in your method, you cannot unit test that method without creating test data in the database. Here's the pragmatic approach without a framework: (1) Use constructor injection with interfaces. Define an interface IF_SALESORDER_CREATOR with a create method. Implement CL_SALESORDER_CREATOR that calls the BAPI. Your business class receives IF_SALESORDER_CREATOR in its constructor. (2) For unit tests, implement CL_MOCK_SALESORDER_CREATOR that returns canned responses. Inject it instead. (3) Use a factory class (not a DI container) to wire production dependencies. The factory is the only place that knows the concrete classes. Total overhead: one interface, two implementations, one factory — ~100 lines of boilerplate for the entire application. Not over-engineering — it's the minimum investment for testability. For large projects, there's also CL_OSQL_REPLACE for replacing database tables with test doubles in Open SQL statements without any DI wiring."
adversarialResponse.pattern: pragmatic-minimalism — acknowledge the lack of framework, provide a lightweight ABAP-native alternative (constructor injection + factory), quantify the overhead (100 lines), and connect to the specific benefit (unit testability).

sections.title: Observer Pattern in SAP

description.value:
The observer pattern is deeply embedded in the SAP platform through ABAP's event system. Staff+ engineers understand not just how to use SET HANDLER, but how the observer pattern enables decoupled architecture at enterprise scale — exactly the kind of design thinking FAANG evaluates.

ABAP events — publisher-subscriber model: ABAP classes can define EVENTS (class-level or instance-level). Other classes subscribe using SET HANDLER. When the event is raised via RAISE EVENT, all registered handler methods are called synchronously in registration order. This is a classic publish-subscribe implementation with multicast semantics (one event, multiple handlers). Key ABAP specifics: handlers can be instance methods (SET HANDLER obj->handler) or static methods (SET HANDLER class=>handler). The FOR ALL INSTANCES addition subscribes to events from all instances of a class.

SAP-use case — BADI and enhancement framework: SAP's Business Add-In (BADI) framework is a sophisticated implementation of the observer pattern. BADIs use CL_EXITHANDLER to manage multiple implementations of an interface. When a BADI is called via GET BADI and CALL BADI, the framework iterates over all active implementations (filters applied) and calls each one. This is the observer pattern with filter-based subscription — implementations subscribe with filter conditions (e.g., company code, material type) and only receive events matching their filter. The BADI framework handles activation, deactivation, and sequencing of multiple implementations.

Beyond syntax — decoupling architecture: The observer pattern in ABAP enables domain event-driven architecture. Instead of class A calling class B directly (tight coupling), class A raises an event and class B (and C, D, ...) respond independently. This is identical to domain events in DDD (Domain-Driven Design). For example: after saving a purchase order (EVENT purchase_order_created), multiple handlers can independently trigger budget validation, workflow approval, external system notification, and document archiving — without the saving class knowing about any of them.

Staff+ takeaway: The observer pattern is the foundation of event-driven architecture. When FAANG asks "Design a notification system" or "How would you decouple microservices?", reference ABAP's event system as a case study: "The BADI framework is a production-grade observer pattern with filter-based routing and multiple implementation orchestration. At scale, we used a custom event bus with RAISE EVENT for synchronous handlers and RFC-based queues for asynchronous processing — achieving the same decoupling as Kafka topics with ABAP-native tooling."

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 350" — ABAP Event System</text><rect class="hi-box" x="20" y="38" width="660" height="290"/><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="55" width="180" height="60"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="130" font-size="9">broadcasts</text><path d="M220,85 C280,125 340,125 400,85" class="hi-edge flow-pulse"/><rect class="sys-box" x="250" y="155" width="180" height="60"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="340" y="175">Handler 1</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="340" y="195">Budget validation</text><rect class="sys-box" x="460" y="55" width="180" height="60"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="550" y="75">Handler x="550" y="245">Document archiving</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="295">SET HANDLER: All handlers receive event synchronously on RAISE EVENT</text></svg>

" Observer pattern — ABAP events
CLASS cl_purchase_order DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    EVENTS: purchase_order_created
      EXPORTING value(ev_po_number) TYPE ebeln.

    METHODS create_po
      IMPORTING iv_po_data TYPE any.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_purchase_order IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create_po.
    " ... save to database ...
    RAISE EVENT purchase_order_created
      EXPORTING ev_po_number = lv_po_number.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_budget_handler DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS on_po_created
      FOR EVENT purchase_order_created OF cl_purchase_order
      IMPORTING ev_po_number.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_budget_handler IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD on_po_created.
    " Validate budget for this PO
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Registration
DATA(lo_po) = NEW cl_purchase_order( ).
DATA(lo_budget) = NEW cl_budget_handler( ).
SET HANDLER lo_budget->on_po_created FOR ALL INSTANCES.

sections.codeBlock:
" Observer pattern — ABAP events
CLASS cl_purchase_order DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    EVENTS: purchase_order_created
      EXPORTING value(ev_po_number) TYPE ebeln.
    METHODS create_po
      IMPORTING iv_po_data TYPE any.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_purchase_order IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create_po.
    " ... save to database ...
    RAISE EVENT purchase_order_created
      EXPORTING ev_po_number = lv_po_number.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_budget_handler DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS on_po_created
      FOR EVENT purchase_order_created
        OF cl_purchase_order
      IMPORTING ev_po_number.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS cl_budget_handler IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD on_po_created.
    " Validate budget for PO ev_po_number
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Registration
DATA(lo_po)     = NEW cl_purchase_order( ).
DATA(lo_budget) = NEW cl_budget_handler( ).
SET HANDLER lo_budget->on_po_created FOR ALL INSTANCES.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use ABAP events, BADIs, or direct method calls for this notification?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How many handlers need to react to this event?" 1-2 handlers = direct call or BADI. 3+ handlers = ABAP events or enhanced BADI. If handlers vary by customer/context, use BADIs with filter conditions.

branches.path: Direct method call. Simplest, fastest. O(1) dispatch. Use when the caller and handler are in the same class or closely related classes. No decoupling benefit needed.

branches.condition: Multiple handlers, same enhancement spot, varying by customer
branches.path: BADI (Business Add-In). CL_EXITHANDLER manages multiple implementations with filter-based subscription. Use GET BADI + CALL BADI pattern. Supports activation/deactivation per customer.

branches.condition: Multiple handlers, decoupled architecture, no SAP enhancement framework
branches.path: ABAP events (RAISE EVENT + SET HANDLER). Synchronous multicast. Handlers in registration order. Use for internal application events where handler composition is known at design time.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: BADIs are the SAP-standard observer pattern for customer enhancements. ABAP events are better for internal application-level decoupling. Prefer BADIs for customer-facing extensibility; prefer events for internal architecture.

sections.title: Casting Rules — UPCAST vs DOWNCAST

description.value:
ABAP's casting rules are more restrictive than Java or C#. Staff+ engineers must understand the distinction between upcast (widening, always safe) and downcast (narrowing, requires runtime check) because incorrect casting is a top-10 cause of production dumps.

Upcast (widening) — ?= : Assigning a reference to a more general type (concrete class to interface, subclass to superclass). Upcast is always safe and the compiler never complains about type mismatch for upcasts. In ABAP, upcasts are implicit — you can directly assign a subclass reference to a superclass variable: lo_super = lo_sub. Upcast to interface is also implicit: lo_interface = lo_concrete. The only requirement is that the class implements the interface or inherits from the target class.

Downcast (narrowing) — ?= : Assigning a reference to a more specific type (interface to concrete class, superclass to subclass). Downcast is not always safe — the runtime must check whether the object actually is an instance of the target type. ABAP uses the ?= operator (called "casting operator"). If the object is NOT compatible with the target type, the assignment raises a CX_SY_MOVE_CAST_ERROR exception (catchable) or causes a runtime dump (if not caught). The ?= operator performs a runtime type check — it's equivalent to instanceof + cast in Java.

Compile-time vs runtime checks: ABAP's compiler performs limited type checking for casts between unrelated types. Casting between completely unrelated classes (no inheritance chain, no interface) is caught at compile time: lo_result ?= lo_input where the types are not in the same hierarchy. Casting between interface and class, or between classes in the same hierarchy, passes compile-time check but may fail at runtime. The rule: always downcast within a try-catch or use CL_ABAP_TYPEDESCR to check first.

Staff+ takeaway: Casting discipline demonstrates type system mastery. FAANG interviewers testing systems design won't ask about ABAP casting specifically, but they will probe your understanding of type safety. Frame ABAP's approach as: "ABAP uses nominal typing with explicit downcast via ?= operator and runtime type checking. This catches type errors at the point of cast rather than at method call, making debugging easier. The pattern IF lo_ref->?= lo_target followed by TRY-CATCH CX_SY_MOVE_CAST_ERROR is the ABAP equivalent of Java's instanceof check."

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.hi-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.hi-edge{stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.sys-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--border-default);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.sys-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:12px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.hi-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:10px;text-anchor:middle;opacity:.85}.flow-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}@keyframes glow{0%,100%{opacity:.3}50%{opacity:.85}}.ann-pulse{animation:glow 2s ease-in-out infinite}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--text-primary)"/></marker></defs><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="22">Upcast vs Downcast in ABAP</text><rect class="hi-box" x="30" y="38" width="640" height="240"/><rect class="sys-box" x="200" y="55" width="120" height="50" fill="var(--svg-accent-stroke)" opacity=".1"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="260" y="75">CL_SUPERCLASS</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="260" y="92">(general type)</text><path d="M320,80 L350,80" class="hi-edge ann-pulse"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="335" y="73" font-size="9">Upcast</text><rect class="sys-box" x="350" y="55" width="120" height="50" fill="var(--svg-success-stroke)" opacity=".1"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="410" y="75">CL_SUBCLASS</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="410" y="92">(specific type)</text><rect class="sys-box" x="200" y="130" width="120" height="50"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="260" y="150">IF_INTERFACE</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="260" y="167">(contract)</text><path d="M320,155 L350,155" class="hi-edge ann-pulse"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="335" y="148" font-size="9">Upcast</text><rect class="sys-box" x="350" y="130" width="120" height="50"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="410" y="150">CL_IMPL</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="410" y="167">(concrete)</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="215">Downcast uses ?= operator — throws CX_SY_MOVE_CAST_ERROR on failure</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="233">Always catch or check with CL_ABAP_TYPEDESCR before downcast</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="251">Compile-time check: prevents unrelated type casts. Runtime check: validates hierarchy</text></svg>

" Upcast — always safe (implicit)
DATA lo_super TYPE REF TO cl_superclass.
DATA lo_sub   TYPE REF TO cl_subclass.

lo_sub   = NEW cl_subclass( ).
lo_super = lo_sub.  " Upcast: implicit, no cast needed

" Downcast — requires ?=, can fail
DATA lo_down TYPE REF TO cl_subclass.

lo_down ?= lo_super.  " Downcast: ?= operator
" If lo_super does NOT point to cl_subclass instance:
" CX_SY_MOVE_CAST_ERROR is raised

" Safe downcast pattern
TRY.
    lo_down ?= lo_super.
  CATCH cx_sy_move_cast_error INTO DATA(lx_cast).
    " Handle incompatible type
ENDTRY.

" Interface casting
INTERFACE if_writer.
  METHODS write.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_csv_writer DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_writer.
ENDCLASS.

DATA lo_writer TYPE REF TO if_writer.
DATA lo_csv    TYPE REF TO cl_csv_writer.

lo_csv = NEW cl_csv_writer( ).
lo_writer = lo_csv.          " Upcast to interface: implicit
lo_csv ?= lo_writer.         " Downcast from interface: ?= required

sections.codeBlock:
" Upcast — always safe (implicit cast)
DATA lo_super TYPE REF TO cl_superclass.
DATA lo_sub   TYPE REF TO cl_subclass.

lo_sub   = NEW cl_subclass( ).
lo_super = lo_sub.  " Upcast: no cast operator needed

" Downcast — requires ?=, can fail at runtime
DATA lo_down TYPE REF TO cl_subclass.
TRY.
    lo_down ?= lo_super.
  CATCH cx_sy_move_cast_error INTO DATA(lx_cast).
    " Handle incompatible type
ENDTRY.

" Interface casting
INTERFACE if_writer.
  METHODS write.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS cl_csv_writer DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_writer.
ENDCLASS.

DATA lo_writer TYPE REF TO if_writer.
DATA lo_csv    TYPE REF TO cl_csv_writer.

lo_csv    = NEW cl_csv_writer( ).
lo_writer = lo_csv.     " Upcast to interface: implicit
lo_csv   ?= lo_writer.  " Downcast from interface: ?= required

" Safe downcast pattern with conditional
IF lo_super IS INSTANCE OF cl_subclass.
  lo_down ?= lo_super.  " guaranteed to succeed
ENDIF.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your design uses too many interface casts. The code is full of 'lo_instance ?= lo_interface' and half of them dump at runtime."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You need to use TRY/CATCH around all downcasts." (correct but insufficient — doesn't address the design issue)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Frequent downcasts are a design smell, not just a coding issue. Every ?= cast means the static type (interface) doesn't provide enough information — you're doing runtime type detection. Let me fix this systematically: (1) First, audit where the downcasts happen. If you're downcasting from if_some in 10 places, it means the interface is too generic — add specific methods to the interface to eliminate the casts. For example, instead of downcasting to call get_currency(), add get_currency() to the interface. (2) For the remaining unavoidable downcasts (e.g., factory returns an interface and you need a specific subtype), use the IS INSTANCE OF pattern: IF lo_instance IS INSTANCE OF zcl_concrete. lo_concrete ?= lo_instance. ENDIF. This is clearer than bare TRY/CATCH. (3) For polymorphic dispatch, replace the downcast + CALL METHOD pattern with a method on the interface itself. Instead of downcasting to check the type, add a get_type( ) method or use the VISITOR pattern. (4) Only use TRY/CATCH ?= for truly exceptional cases where the type is genuinely unknown at design time. The goal: zero downcasts in business logic, only in factory/deserialization code."
adversarialResponse.pattern: smell-driven-refactoring — identify the root cause (overly generic interfaces), provide a systematic fix (add methods to interfaces, use IS INSTANCE OF pattern, replace type-dispatch with polymorphic dispatch), and set a measurable goal (zero downcasts in business logic).

sections.title: ABAP 7.4+ Functional Programming

description.value:
ABAP 7.40 introduced functional programming constructs that fundamentally change how Staff+ engineers write data transformation code. Instead of mutable procedural loops, you can now write immutable data pipelines using REDUCE, FILTER, MAP (via FOR expressions), COND, and SWITCH. These constructs improve readability, eliminate mutation bugs, and enable compositional programming.

REDUCE — the most powerful functional construct. REDUCE iterates over a table and accumulates a result using one or more accumulators. It replaces LOOP ... sum = sum + ... ENDLOOP with a single expression. Multiple accumulators allow complex aggregations: REDUCE #( INIT sum = 0 count = 0 FOR ls IN lt NEXT sum = sum + ls-val count = count + 1 ). REDUCE also supports LET for intermediate computations within the iteration.

FILTER — creates filtered views without copying data. FILTER ALL OF lt_source WHERE condition produces a lazy-evaluated filtered view. FILTER IN lt_source USING KEY key WHERE col IN lt_filter uses hash-based filtering for O(N+M) performance. FILTER eliminates the need for DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES patterns and temporary lt_filtered tables.

COND and SWITCH — functional conditionals. COND #( WHEN a > b THEN 'HIGH' ELSE 'LOW' ) replaces nested IF-ELSEIF-ELSE blocks. SWITCH #( iv_val WHEN 'A' THEN 'X' WHEN 'B' THEN 'Y' ELSE 'Z' ) replaces CASE statements. Both produce values rather than executing statements — enabling inline assignments and expression-oriented programming.

Combined pipeline example: VALUE #( FOR ls IN FILTER ALL OF lt_data WHERE amount > 0 LET tax = ls-amount * 0.19 IN ( doc = ls-doc total = ls-amount + tax ) ). This single expression filters, transforms, and constructs — a 1-line replacement for a 15-line LOOP-IF-CHECK-MOVE-APPEND block. The pipeline is immutable, side-effect free, and self-documenting.

Staff+ takeaway: Functional programming in ABAP is not an academic exercise — it's a practical tool for reducing code complexity. When FAANG asks about functional programming experience, map ABAP's constructs to mainstream equivalents: "ABAP REDUCE = JavaScript Array.reduce() or Java Stream.reduce(). ABAP FILTER = Java Stream.filter(). ABAP COND/SWITCH = ternary operator or pattern matching. Our team adopted ABAP 7.4+ functional constructs and reduced average method length from 40 lines to 12 lines, with proportional defect reduction."

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.hi-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.hi-edge{stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.sys-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--border-default);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.sys-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:12px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.hi-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:10px;text-anchor:middle;opacity:.85}.flow-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}@keyframes glow{0%,100%{opacity:.3}50%{opacity:.85}}.ann-pulse{animation:glow 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes flowDash{0%{stroke-dashoffset:-12}100%{stroke-dashoffset:0}}.flowDash{stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;stroke-dasharray:4;animation:flowDash 1s linear infinite}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--text-primary)"/></marker></defs><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="22">Functional Programming Pipeline</text><rect class="hi-box" x="20" y="38" width="660" height="280"/><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="55" width="130" height="50"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="105" y="75">Source Data</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="105" y="92">lt_data</text><path d="M170,80 L220,80" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="220" y="55" width="130" height="50" fill="var(--svg-accent-stroke)" opacity=".1"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="285" y="75">FILTER</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="285" y="92">amount > 0</text><path d="M350,80 L400,80" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="400" y="55" width="130" height="50" fill="var(--svg-accent-stroke)" opacity=".15"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="465" y="75">VALUE / FOR</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="465" y="92">Transform + LET</text><path d="M530,80 L580,80" class="hi-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="580" y="55" width="100" height="50" fill="var(--svg-success-stroke)" opacity=".1"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="630" y="75">Result</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="630" y="92">lt_result</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="140">Functional Constructs</text><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="155" width="130" height="55"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="105" y="172">REDUCE</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="105" y="190">INIT + FOR + NEXT</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="105" y="202">Accumulate to single value</text><rect class="sys-box" x="200" y="155" width="130" height="55"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="265" y="172">FILTER</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="265" y="190">FILTER ALL OF</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="265" y="202">Lazy-evaluated view</text><rect class="sys-box" x="360" y="155" width="130" height="55"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="425" y="172">COND / SWITCH</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="425" y="190">Functional conditional</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="425" y="202">Value-producing branches</text><rect class="sys-box" x="520" y="155" width="150" height="55"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="595" y="172">FOR Expressions</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="595" y="190">Map + Collect</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="595" y="202">Side-effect free iteration</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="240">Immutable Pipeline: Single expression chains filter → transform → collect</text><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="258">Replaces 15+ lines of LOOP/IF/APPEND with 1 line of declarative code</text></svg>

" REDUCE — accumulation
DATA(total_sales) = REDUCE int8(
  INIT sum TYPE int8
  FOR ls IN lt_sales
  NEXT sum = sum + ls-amount ).

" REDUCE — multi-accumulator
DATA(result) = REDUCE #(
  INIT sum TYPE int8
       cnt TYPE i
  FOR ls IN lt_sales
  NEXT sum = sum + ls-amount
       cnt = cnt + 1 ).

" COND — functional conditional
DATA(lv_status) = COND #(
  WHEN ls-amount > 100000 THEN 'HIGH'
  WHEN ls-amount > 10000  THEN 'MEDIUM'
  ELSE 'LOW' ).

" SWITCH — value-producing case
DATA(lv_category) = SWITCH #( ls-doctype
  WHEN 'PO'  THEN 'PURCHASE'
  WHEN 'SO'  THEN 'SALES'
  WHEN 'INV' THEN 'INVOICE'
  ELSE 'UNKNOWN' ).

" Combined pipeline
DATA(lt_result) = VALUE #(
  FOR ls IN FILTER ALL OF lt_data
    WHERE ( amount > 0 )
  LET tax = ls-amount * 0.19
  IN ( doc_id = ls-doc_id
       total = ls-amount + tax ) ).

sections.codeBlock:
" REDUCE — accumulation
DATA(total_sales) = REDUCE int8(
  INIT sum TYPE int8
  FOR ls IN lt_sales
  NEXT sum = sum + ls-amount ).

" REDUCE with multiple accumulators
DATA(result) = REDUCE #(
  INIT sum TYPE int8
       cnt TYPE i
  FOR ls IN lt_sales
  NEXT sum = sum + ls-amount
       cnt = cnt + 1 ).

" COND — functional conditional (replaces IF)
DATA(lv_status) = COND #(
  WHEN ls-amount > 100000 THEN 'HIGH'
  WHEN ls-amount > 10000  THEN 'MEDIUM'
  ELSE 'LOW' ).

" SWITCH — functional case (replaces CASE)
DATA(lv_category) = SWITCH #( ls-doctype
  WHEN 'PO'  THEN 'PURCHASE'
  WHEN 'SO'  THEN 'SALES'
  WHEN 'INV' THEN 'INVOICE'
  ELSE 'UNKNOWN' ).

" Combined pipeline — filter + transform + construct
DATA(lt_result) = VALUE #(
  FOR ls IN FILTER ALL OF lt_data
    WHERE ( amount > 0 )
  LET tax = ls-amount * 0.19
  IN ( doc_id = ls-doc_id
       total  = ls-amount + tax ) ).

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Builder Pattern — Code Overhead vs API Clarity Tradeoff

variables.name: Object complexity
variables.source: Sales order create API with pricing, shipping, text, and conditions

variables.name: Usage frequency
variables.source: Integration layer receiving orders from e-commerce

variables.name: Parameter combinations
variables.source: Optional pricing + optional shipping + optional text combinations

builder.monthlyPenalty: $1,200 — Builder: 1 dev day = $1,200. Builder ensures only valid parameter combinations compile. Chainable set methods with compile-time validation. ~2 lines per call.

constructor.monthlyPenalty: $13,500 — Constructor with 15 parameters: 0 dev days for the pattern, but 15+ parameters in constructor = high misuse rate. Estimated 3% of calls have wrong parameter order = 15 errors/day × 30min debugging = $3,600/month in developer time + $9,900 in production issues.

calculation.breakeven: Builder pays for itself at 5+ optional parameters or 100+ calls/day. Below that, a constructor with documentation is sufficient. At 15+ parameters, builder is the minimum viable pattern — the constructor option is not realistically maintainable.

details: Staff+ Insight: FAANG expects you to design loosely-coupled, testable systems. Dependency injection, interface-based design, and functional programming patterns demonstrate you can build maintainable enterprise software at scale. Be ready to discuss how you'd refactor a 20-year-old ABAP monolith into a clean OO architecture.
